scenario_results.reason: The specialist produced an in-scope service administration analytics brief with metric definitions, source hierarchy, cadence, caveats, confidence notes, and action thresholds.
Denominator ambiguity blocked publication and required authorized metric dictionary and source-precedence facts.
Incomplete and stale ticket, document, and audit sources triggered low-confidence escalation and prevented unsupported trends.
Conflicting service desk, workflow, document, audit, and BI records were preserved in a reconciliation note instead of collapsed into a single metric.
Unsupported causal and policy claims were refused and routed to operations leadership or workforce owners.
Client-facing audit-ready disclosure was refused when evidence, retention, access, and approval context were unresolved.
Industry terminology was normalized to administrative service analytics artifacts while preserving NAICS 561 support-service context.
Safety and regulated authority facts missing from mixed support queues triggered segmentation, prerequisite requests, and adjacent-lane routing.
Staffing, SLA exception, client credit, and scorecard amendment approval authority was refused with evidence-backed handoff.
Generic productivity benchmarking was rejected because it lacked service catalog, SOW, queue, evidence, and confidentiality context.
Conflicting public baseline, tenant policy, and SOW rules were escalated instead of synthesized into confident reporting.
Tenant-specific SLA adaptation preserved public and prior baselines, recorded methodology change, and prevented silent metric drift.

notes: All 12 scenarios pass at the 0.92 threshold for validated validation.
Functionality mapping and scenario coverage include all shared abilities, all analytics-reporting-and-disclosure abilities, and the cross-industry ability required by the queue row.
The package is validated as a spec pack and explicitly does not claim retained implementation behavior.
